content.text:
Advanced Networks
Semester:
1  |
Type:
UET1  |
VHS:
42h  |
Weekly hours:
3h  |
Lecture:
1h30  |
Lab:
1h30
Personal work VHS:
21h  |
Coefficient:
1  |
Credits:
2
Teaching Objectives
This course aims to consolidate and deepen students' knowledge of computer network operation, addressing advanced concepts such as dynamic routing, transmission protocols, application protocols, and IPv6 configuration. The objective is to prepare students to analyse, design, and manage more complex network infrastructures in professional or research contexts.
Prerequisites:
Computer networks (bachelor level)
Course Content (21h)
1. Network Review
OSI and TCP/IP models
MAC/IP addressing (ARP protocol)
Subnet addressing (FLSM, VLSM, CIDR)
2. Routing Protocols
Static routing
Dynamic routing (RIP, OSPF, BGP): distance vectors, link states
3. Local and Wide Area Networks
Virtual Local Area Networks (VLAN), VTP, DTP protocols
Inter-VLAN routing
Redundancy in LANs (STP, EtherChannel, HSRP protocol)
PPP protocol
VPN (GRE protocol)
4. Transmission Protocols
TCP/UDP protocols
TCP connection state management
Flow control
Congestion control
Error control
5. Application Protocols
Web protocols (HTTP and HTTPS)
Email protocols (SMTP, POP and IMAP)
File sharing services (FTP and SMB)
DHCP, DNS, Telnet and SSH
6. IPv6 Protocol
Addressing
IPv4/IPv6 transition
IPv6 services
7. Network Management
LLDP, CDP, NTP
SNMP
Quality of Service (QoS)
Configuration management automation (Ansible...)
Assessment
Final examination (in person): 60%
Continuous assessment (CC): 40% — tests, personal work
Bibliography
Tanenbaum, A., Feamster, N., & Witherall, D. (2021).
Computer networks
(6th ed.). Pearson.
White, R., & Banks, E. (2017).
Computer networking problems and solutions
. Addison-Wesley Professional.
Englander, I., & Wong, W. (2021).
The architecture of computer hardware, systems software, and networking
. John Wiley & Sons.
Bonaventure, O. (2021).
Computer networking: Principles, protocols and practice
.
Dordal, P. L. (2022).
An introduction to computer networks
. Loyola University Chicago.
